name: Reduce GraphQL resolver time for relationship traversal into SQLite queries

description: Baseline before changes for GraphQL resolver relationship traversal into SQLite queries.

Batched preview structured-text materialization across pending resolver requests and nested block fields, but TypeScript checks failed from a stale single-call reference in src/graphql/structured-text-loader.ts.

Batch structured-text preview materialization by ancestry group so one loader flush fetches block rows across all pending roots and nested block fields together instead of materializing each request sequentially.

Tried running per-block-table materialization queries concurrently within each ancestry batch using Effect.forEach({ concurrency: 'unbounded' }); it regressed total_ms, likely from D1/SqlClient contention and scheduling overhead.

Cache candidate block-model table lists inside the shared StructuredText materialization context so batched preview traversal reuses the same allowed-table filter results across nested ancestry groups.
Precompute each parsed StructuredText request's block-id Set once and reuse it across ancestry-batch dispatch, removing repeated Set allocation during batched preview materialization.

Tried persisting a shared StructuredText materialization context on each request-scoped loader to reuse schema/table caches across flushes, but it regressed total_ms; the extra loader state and longer-lived caches did not pay off on this benchmark.
Tried a single-pass DAST id collector plus per-call block typename caching inside src/graphql/structured-text-resolver.ts, but it regressed total_ms versus the current best.

Tried replacing recursive nested StructuredText materialization with an iterative breadth-first work queue that builds nested envelope placeholders inline; it regressed, likely from extra parsing and queue bookkeeping on each nested block field.
Tried Promise.all collection for linked-record loader/cache result assembly instead of awaiting cached promises in sequence, but it regressed total_ms and preview_deep_ms.
Re-ran the current best commit to check noise after several regressions; result was slower than the 210.378ms best, so keep the earlier baseline for subsequent experiments.

Tried precomputing per-block-model StructuredText field metadata so batched preview materialization could reuse allowed block whitelists and field descriptors, but the extra schema shape and indirection regressed total_ms.

Tried routing block-level draft structured-text fields through the request-scoped StructuredText envelope loader before resolveStructuredTextValue, but it regressed total_ms and did not help the benchmark workload.
Tried slimming parsed StructuredText request objects by copying only the materialization params and dropping nested per-request materializeContext references, but it regressed total_ms.

Tried precomputing all GraphQL content model api keys once per schema build and passing them into StructuredText link resolution instead of remapping models on every resolver call, but it regressed total_ms.
Tried WeakMap caching for getBlockWhitelist() validator decoding in src/db/validators.ts, but it regressed total_ms on the resolver benchmark.

Tried using D1 driver-level batch() for per-table StructuredText block fetches inside the hot preview loop; benchmark improved, but TypeScript checks failed because the helper's generic row type was not constrained to object.
Fixed the D1 batch() prototype for per-table StructuredText block fetches and reran; with checks passing it regressed badly, so the earlier apparent speedup was not stable and this path is discarded.
Tried selecting only the block materialization columns (id, root columns, and field columns) instead of SELECT * in StructuredText preview block fetches, but it regressed total_ms.

Tried prewarming all block model schemas in one fields-table query per materialization context; benchmark improved, but TypeScript failed because MaterializeContext candidateBlockModels/new Map initializers still used the old api_key-only type.

Fixed and reran the block-schema prewarm idea (load all block model field schemas in one query per materialization context). It reduced some preview-deep work but regressed overall total_ms, so discard.

Use direct D1 prepare().bind().all() for the hot StructuredText preview block fetch loop in src/services/structured-text-service.ts, falling back to SqlClient elsewhere; this trims Effect SQL wrapper overhead on the hottest per-table reads.

Tried extending direct D1 raw reads from the hot block fetch loop to structured-text schema lookups (models/fields), but the refactor broke Effect context typing around compileStructuredText and checks failed.

Tried module-level prepared statement reuse on top of the direct-D1 hot read path in src/services/structured-text-service.ts, but it regressed badly; D1's own behavior/cache appears better than manual WeakMap caching here.

Tried extending direct-D1 reads to block metadata lookups (models/fields/fetchBlockModels), but checks failed from Effect environment typing and unused placeholder bindings; also benchmarked worse, so this direction looks weak.

Tried field-aware JSON decoding in StructuredText block payload materialization to skip generic deserialize work on non-JSON strings, but checks failed because the old deserializeValue helper became unused; benchmark also looked worse than the current best.

Tried extending the direct-D1 path only to fetchBlockModels() while preserving Effect signatures; checks passed but total_ms regressed, so even this adjacent metadata read is not worth moving off SqlClient.
Tried extending direct-D1 only to the block-model lookup inside getBlockModelSchema(), but it both regressed and broke Effect context typing around the structured-text compile path.
Precomputed StructuredText field block whitelists once per GraphQL resolver closure in content/block resolvers instead of calling getBlockWhitelist() per record, but it regressed total_ms.
Simplified ancestry-batch dispatch by dropping the extra requestKey→blockIdSet map and reusing request.blockIdSet directly, but total_ms still regressed.
Tried extending direct D1 reads to GraphQL linked-record content fetches in src/graphql/structured-text-resolver.ts, but it regressed total_ms and preview_deep_ms versus the current best.
Tried replacing the ancestry-batch rootRecordId Map with a plain object lookup in StructuredText materialization, but it regressed badly (including published queries), so keep the Map-based version.

Shared one StructuredText materialization context across all structured_text fields in materializeRecordStructuredTextFields() for a record instead of creating one per field, but it regressed total_ms on the benchmark.

Tried resolving D1 availability once per StructuredText materialization and passing the db into the hot block query helper, but checks failed on a nullability issue and the benchmark was slower anyway.
Tried sharing only the StructuredText block-model schema cache across a whole GraphQL request via GqlContext while keeping per-flush candidate table caches local, but it regressed badly.

Tried adding a WeakMap cache keyed by the allowedBlockApiKeys array to avoid repeated join()/filter work when selecting candidate block tables, but checks failed (loader materializeContext shape was incomplete) and the benchmark output looked clearly incorrect with much smaller response bytes, so do not trust this run.
Re-ran the current best commit to measure variance after the recent failed ideas; this sample was much slower than the 208.369ms best, so keep the existing best as the baseline.

Tried extending direct-D1 only to the fields-table lookup in getParsedFields(); it regressed and also broke Effect environment inference in compileStructuredText, so this last adjacent metadata-read path looks not worth pursuing.
Special-cased the no-whitelist path in candidate block-model selection to avoid join/map work for the common '*' case, but it regressed badly.
Tried using null-prototype objects for StructuredText envelope blocks maps to reduce hot-path property overhead, but it regressed overall query time.
